---
title: "12.04 LTS Boot full"
date: 2013-01-19
forum: Desktop Environments
---

### Post by SteveTyrer on 2013-01-19
I am running 12.04 LTS, it is set up with 4 HD using LV, I am getting a message saying that Boot is full (it has 256 Mib), I have used Computer Janitor to delete old versions of the kernel with no improvement.
I am a bit unsure about using Gparted to give more space as It will not work with a multi disk  set-up, can some one please give advise on the best way to give the Boot folder more space.

Steve

---

### Post by BlinkinCat on 2013-01-19
> **SteveTyrer said:**
> I am running 12.04 LTS, it is set up with 4 HD using LVD, I am getting a message saying that Boot is full (it has 256 gib), I have used Computer Janitor to delete old versions of the kernel with no improvement.
I am a bit unsure about using Gparted to give more space as It will not work with a multi disk  set-up, can some one please give advise on the best way to give the Boot folder more space.

Steve

Hi,

You do mean boot has 256 MiB don't you and not 256 Gib ?

---

### Post by SteveTyrer on 2013-01-19
oops yes 256 Mib at the start but now zero by the warning message.
If I look at Boot with Gparted it says I have 5.4 Mib free? so why am I get a warning that boot is full?

---

### Post by mcduck on 2013-01-19
256MB is hardly large enough for /boot, and with only an few MB's left you are so close to 100% full that the warning is definitely appropriate.

Anyway, is there a specific reason why you even have a separate /boot? It's rarely needed these days, and in most cases just complicates things for no benefits.

(Also, gparted reports available space on the filesystem, while looking at things in a file manager, for example, reports space available *for current user*. 5% of space on Ext filesystems is by default reserved for root only, and therefore doesn't count as available space when viewing as normal user.)

---

### Post by SteveTyrer on 2013-01-19
> **mcduck said:**
> 256MB is hardly large enough for /boot, and with only an few MB's left you are so close to 100% full that the warning is definitely appropriate.

Anyway, is there a specific reason why you even have a separate /boot? It's rarely needed these days, and in most cases just complicates things for no benefits.

(Also, gparted reports available space on the filesystem, while looking at things in a file manager, for example, reports space available *for current user*. 5% of space on Ext filesystems is by default reserved for root only, and therefore doesn't count as available space when viewing as normal user.)

Thanks for your response,
I only have a separate boot because I was told it is easier to recover stored data if the system crashes!
Anyway what do you recommend I do to correct this issue? (I am not an expert on this sort of thing)

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-01-19
I do not have a separate partition for /boot but use about 124M per du with 5 kernels still installed. While 256MB is not huge it should work. Did you empty trash and since /boot that would be root's trash.
      
gksudo nautilus /root/.local/share/Trash/files  # Be sure to enable viewing of hidden files.

    I thought with LVM there was no way to recover if on partition crashes. Only good backups to another device work.

       Advantages/Disadvantages LVM Post #9 (And he says 200MB should be plenty for /boot.)
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1586328](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1586328)
lvm info older:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=141900](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=141900)
[http://tldp.org/HOWTO/LVM-HOWTO/index.html](http://tldp.org/HOWTO/LVM-HOWTO/index.html)

---

I have looked in the boot file (please see attachment), it looks like I have lots of old kernels, or am I missing something?

---

### Post by oldfred on 2013-01-19
Your list did not attach. 

I usually keep two when I houseclean. Current and one older just in case. I use synaptic.
       Determine your current kernel:
uname -a
uname -r
In synaptic or software center search for linux-image to choose to purge old ones

 A easy way to remove the amount of kernels is to install Ubuntu tweak
[http://ubuntu-tweak.com/](http://ubuntu-tweak.com/)

 HOWTO: Remove Older Kernels via GUI (or CLI) 
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1587462](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1587462)
[http://www.liberiangeek.net/2010/07/remove-kernel-ubuntu-10-04-lucid-lynx-grub/](http://www.liberiangeek.net/2010/07/remove-kernel-ubuntu-10-04-lucid-lynx-grub/)

    Also command line in post #8
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1283521](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1283521)

---
